items.q: Which is larger and more complex, a tissue or an organ?
items.correct: An organ is larger and more complex; it is made of several different tissues working together.
items.misconception: A tissue is larger and more complex than an organ.
items.random_wrong: Tissues and organs are exactly the same thing.
items.why: Students mix up the order of the levels of organisation.

items.q: Are all the cells in a tissue identical types?
items.correct: A tissue is a group of similar, specialised cells working together for one function, such as muscle tissue.
items.misconception: A tissue contains every different type of cell in the body.
items.random_wrong: A tissue is made of just one single cell.
items.why: Students misjudge a tissue as either one cell or a random mixture of all cell types.

items.correct: Magnification equals image size divided by actual size, with both measurements in the same units.
items.misconception: Magnification equals actual size divided by image size.
items.random_wrong: Magnification is the same thing as the microscope's resolution.
items.why: Students invert the formula or confuse magnification with resolution.

items.q: The correct order of organisation from smallest to largest is:
items.why: Correct answer: cell, tissue, organ, organ system. “organ, tissue, cell, organ system” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A group of similar cells working together is a(n):
items.why: It's tissue. “organ” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A root hair cell is specialised for absorption because it has:
items.correct: a long extension giving a large surface area
items.why: The answer examiners want is: a long extension giving a large surface area. “many chloroplasts for photosynthesis” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: If a cell's image is 50 mm and its actual size is 0.05 mm, the magnification is:
items.why: It's x1000. “x100” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: An image of a cell is 50 mm wide and the real cell is 0.05 mm wide. Calculate the magnification.
items.why: The real figure is 1000 times. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
